title: 2026/03/05 - ENV (Part 2 of 2)
description: Thursday, 5th March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Hearing of evidence: Civil Contractors New Zealand Inc Alan Pollard, Chief Executive Fraser May, Communications and Advocacy Manager Bernie Napp, Sustainability & policy director at Concrete NZ IAG New Zealand Bryce Davies, Executive Manager Corporate Relations Andrew Saunders, Government Relations Manager
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Civil Contractors New Zealand Inc

chunks.text: Start back in public session this afternoon on the planning bill and Natural Environment Bill. And we have our first submitter with us, the Civil Contractors New Zealand. Uh, welcome to the select committee this afternoon. It's great to have you with us. Uh, you have 20 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you for your opening remarks and introductions and to talk us through your submission.

But do take it as, uh, read, and of course members will have questions, so we'll hand it over to you. Perfect. Well thanks Madam Chair and committee members. Thanks for the chance to, uh, address you on behalf of civil contract. So we represent about 800 businesses that deliver what we call the backbone of horizontal infrastructure, roads, water, and so on from there.

Um, can I also thank you that chief for allowing us a little bit more time, uh, for our submissions. It's a very complex, but legislation, as you know, we have extensive consultation and we needed to conduct, so we really appreciate that extra time. So thank you for that. So look, I'm Alan Pollard. I'm Chief Executive of Civil Contractors, uh, with me today. First on my left, Fraser May.

He's our communications and advocacy manager. Hard left on the corner there. Uh, Richard Galloway from CCNZ, uh, member Company McConnell Dowell. Uh, and on my right, uh, burning that independent consultant, do cell plus put the submission together as well. So in in general terms, we welcome the introduction of the planning bill and the, uh, natural environment bill.

This is a generational opportunity, and it's one that, uh, does seek to replace some, uh, a management system that becomes frankly unwieldy, uh, over litigious, incredibly falsely for both infrastructure providers, uh, and the communities we serve. As you know, the Infrastructure Commission has just published its, um, infrastructure plan.

It sets out a vision for the next 30 years, uh, for infrastructure delivery. The projects that are contained in those, in that plan, uh, are not optional, not necessary for the benefit of our communities. So our core message really is that in the consenting and compliance framework, we have to be practical, we have to be proportionate, and it has to be workable.

If, uh, we are serious about delivering infrastructure efficiently and affordably to do this and to better protect the environment, we have to reduce complexity. And we've got a right size the system. We support the efforts of the bill to reduce unnecessary consenting for routine civil works and to promote greater standardization across regions.

These are foundational steps to unlocking housing, unlocking regional growth, and eventually productivity. We do, however, strongly caution against any reform that simply replaces ambiguities with more ambiguity. Uh, while the proposed legislation speaks to the issue, in truth, how its application will come into force or being does depend on layers of secondary legislation.

And I'm sure you'll hear all about those from other submitters, uh, as well. So, so for my introductory comments, we support the intent of the build. You have our absolute commitment as an industry and our members that will continue to work constructively with government and all stakeholders to ensure that these changes lead to better outcomes for contractors, for communities, and the environment.

Our members know, and they absolutely accept that they have a critical role in ensuring that any resource management system is implemented successfully. So I'll just pass over to my colleague Fraser to explore a bit more detail. Okay, thanks very much, Alan. Um, Adrian, uh, just like to expand very quickly on, uh, submissions.

We've given you quite a lot of detail in the submission, so we try and leave plenty of time for questions after, so that we can get through any questions you might have about that. I just wanted to give you a couple of examples, uh, about, um, the issues we've been experiencing and then perhaps tie that back to some of the solutions that are proposed in the current legislation. Uh, some things are not going well with the system at the moment.

We hear from members about complexity and cost and actually really poor environmental outcomes that are driven by how some of the regulations have put in place at the moment. A really key example is soil. Uh, so lack of clear definitions, poor, poor application of the standards. And, um, real sort of unwillingness to look at at the practice and, and fa of how, how what the consequences of the regulations are at the moment is putting massive costs into, into infrastructure construction.

And, um, adding around, well, it's, it's been measured at 2.4 billion by wastelands on, um, on top of our infrastructure bill just on moving soil and taking to the tip, which is not something contractors are happy about. They'd rather be reusing, reusing it on site. And, um, the way the standards are put in place at the moment is not enabling that. Um, it's really important to resolve it, but, um, that's just an example of, of of what needs to change if we're gonna be successful in having a system that better protects the environment

and actually is more cost effective doing it. So look, we need to reduce complexity. We need to get it right. Um, significant part of the issue at the moment is that regulations developed without looking at what the practice is first. So, uh, when we heard changes were announced, uh, and, and, and actually that some of the national standards work, so I'm kind of anticipating what comes next through national direction and national standards.

Uh, we kind of thought, well, hmm, that's gonna be a lot of work now had another think about it. And then we thought, well, uh, if it's connected enough, we don't need to make things that much harder for ourselves. Actually, our members do this stuff every day in terms of moving earth, in terms of putting, um, environmental protection in place. And if we can take some of that knowledge and then build it into the system, that that's great.

So for that reason, I've, I've, um, tabled, uh, what we new each a copy of the CNZ Environmental Guide, which shows how those controls can be put in place simply. So it, it's for things like, you know, what de the decibel is a piece of construction machinery might put out. We did have a case in, um, one of the regions where driven piling basically got outlawed by by because they said that it exceeded the decibel limit.

And so if you, but if you've got a short term impact like that, is it in a field? Is it in urban center? You know, those are the sorts of complexities that, um, aren't handled through grant blankets, uh, blanket regulations. So I know, I know that that's kind of been dealt with legislation, but just example. Um, so yeah, that I, I've given you that as an example of where, and, you know, a few pages, we can make it simple for second, uh, coming back to higher level conversation.

But if we can reduce the, the regulatory burden, like we, we'll be able to do things swiftly. And, and I just wanna give you an example of how that could be handled. Um, and, and that was something that members were quite concerned around, which was around the, uh, clause 38 in the planning bill and Clause 39 in the natural environment bill, which where permitted activities must be no rules, permitted activity rules must require written notification to permitting authorities.

And, um, some individual companies might have hundreds of activities they can conducting every day. If you've gotta notify hundreds of permitted activities to permitting authority every day, then that's not gonna be a practical system. It's not gonna reduce complexity. Um, it's over notification. So, I mean, maybe that's just changed a change from the word must to the word make.

Uh, but that's sort of change that is practical and, and that we can actually make at this stage. So I think was 38 and 39, uh, you know, if we can, if we can say that, uh, it's gonna reduce the burden on permitting authorities and on, uh, infrastructure system. So, you know, I hope this drafting error, but it's just an example again, of where we can reduce regulatory complexity, complexity, complexity, get the word right.

Um, and I hope those give you some examples and what we see a work system to be like. Um, in summary, CCCs and supports the reform. We've got a few, yeah, we just a little bit of fine tuning needed around workability clarity and fairness. Um, just like to pass to my colleague Richard Galloway here from McConnell Dowell, and if you wouldn't mind saying a few words, Richard, that'd be great.

Sure. Um, look, I've got a few examples of, of things here and how we can see the, that being supported, but I guess, do you wanna use this time to ask us questions or do you want me to, to run through some examples of what we're seeing at the day? No, don't feel free to run through a couple of examples. Okay, Lovely. Um, well look, I guess first of all, phrase I mentioned clean fills and how, uh, that the soil management, to give you an example, a couple years ago project in Rucker, I had to move 20,000 cubes of dirt.

That's 2000 truck and trailer loads. Closest clean fill I could find to use was our south coast of Wellton. So we moved 2000 truck and trailer loads, burning all the fossil fuels all the way through Brooklyn, past all the shops past into schools, down to the South coast and back out again because there'd been no planning in place to support or facilitate clean fills close to the site or anywhere in the Hu Valley.

So, um, suboptimal. So, so yeah, that's why it's really important we see to get that, that soil management part included. Um, but a couple of things around designations. Yeah. And looking at how it's set up to work with construction plans, uh, fantastic. Um, it does need to be really careful that it makes your designation stay high level, and the level of information that people know about when they're preparing a designation is pretty light.

Uh, and it's only when we work through a project that we start to know more of the detail. Now, I take an example, live, live example right now, um, up towards, up the coast on a, um, a large expressway project we're working on where, uh, a bridge that thought the best solution was to put two bridges close next to each other. Um, those we work through the details, start to understand more about the, um, soil conditions, hydrology conclusions.

It's better to put it together a single bridge, um, conclusion at the moment or advices that that would be a change and it would need to go back through recons consenting. Mm, not within, not within general accordance, uh, when you're running at about a million dollars a day. That's what we are currently running at. That's the project cost at the moment. Uh, something like that, which gets in the way and requires you to have a plan.

And another plan, another plan, um, is obviously really expensive. Um, and, and simply because the original conditions were just too tight and didn't allow for that adaptation as we went through. And I guess on that point of adaptation, when we look at the precautionary principle, which is to be written into this, um, one of the things we see with it is that that adaptive management goes with it and think that's really important to allow both.

I think of examples like West Wind Wind Farm, which, uh, had two access points, uh, and had road upgrades required for both of them. In the end decided to only use one of them, but was still required to do all the road upgrades to the other, the other access point that wasn't even used. So it had to kind of the early precautionary approach of what if, but no adaptive management to say, actually we're not gonna use that, so let's not to save that money, use it better, and essentially make renewable energy cheaper, which would be a good outcome.

Um, I, I guess another part site to support is the, uh, the wildlife permits. So I see that, um, this allows the wildlife permits to be approved in it. I look at a couple of recent projects, um, KU at out mile point, um, the such treatment, um, UA Expressway, both of those we're talking $2 million, $2 billion worth of projects.

The last permit to come through was the wildlife permit. Mm-hmm. It was tense. We were worried we had diggers at the waiting, but essentially, you know, if this doesn't come through today, we are gonna be incurring costs and we are gonna have plant standing, people standing being an efficient, um, no environmental benefit at all. So the idea of wrapping it into one approval process definitely can see savings outta that.

Uh, but there's a few examples hopefully that that helps. Really helpful to put it in context. Uh, absolutely. Um, well, thank you very much for, um, that submission and analysis. Um, look, I might kick off with, uh, some questions just because I was quite interested around the, um, points you made around soil management.

And within your submission here, you've said that you feel that there needs to be more attention in the bills, um, and other legislation to ensure that soil should be able to be reused where possible. Can you just elaborate a little bit further on what you think the solution is? Yeah, I'll start off with that if I could. Uh, so, uh, there, there are a few different issues there.

One is around how can contaminated is defined, and at the moment, a lot of authorities are treating it is, if there's a presence of contaminant, then it needs to be disposed of at a contaminated waste site. However, all soil contains some form of contaminant, really. Like if you've got trace arsenic in the soil in Canterbury, it's basically treated as contaminated just 'cause you pick it up off the ground.

And so instead of, um, being able to reuse it at six to or 60 a ton on, on a neighboring site, or a cont or a, or a clean fill, that, that, you know, that six 60 a ton, you know, that's, that's, that's disposal as well, rather than reuse, we wanna see them reusing it. Like, um, people are basically being forced to truck across the Southern Alps because it's cheaper than taking it to contaminated waste Southern Canterbury.

It's ridiculous. Mm-hmm. Um, so, so that's, that's an example. So that comes from the contaminated, um, soils definitions, and that's something that we haven't got. Right. And that's, I think, sitting within the national environmental standard of, so that's an example of national direction and the interpretation of the national direction, not, not getting quite right.

Uh, waste Men's will have some more comment about that. And I believe you hearing from them, um, in a future submission, uh, and we've been working with them closely on it because we see there's a big problem there and we hear about it from members all time. They'd rather be building infrastructure than, um, carting soil long distances. It's just So do You agree? Do, Do you and Wasteman agree? I I, I think, I think we roughly agree on, on the solution that yes, we need vetted definitions around that, and also we need to acknowledge, um, the other part

of the issue is, is almost a social license issue. So, um, field sites are seen as bad, basically field sites are bad, uh, is kind of the narrative it gets across. And, and actually what we'd love to see is them recognized as as a regional resource. So if you've got, uh, and, and emphasis on, on reusing soil, enabling that through say, hydrovac truck waste, recy recycling, because it's not waste, it's gravel that could be graded, it's sand, it could be graded, recycled, reused.

If it's, um, needs to go to a fill site, those sites should be available in close proximity to, um, to, um, the site where the construction's taking place. Because I mean, as, as Richard says, we might have 20,000 CIC kiers of soil removed from some projects. And if that's not being provided for us, a regional need, regional resource, uh, that then, then we're not gonna be able to do that efficiently.

So yeah, we can, yeah. And often it's being prevented by the regulations from re region on site. So I've actually written it down if anyone's interested in the paper we've written on that. I've actually got a couple of copies here that I'm happy to hand out, and I could even just leave that with, um, the committee if you like. And I'm very happy to circulate that after and, and to discuss it with any of you that wanna discuss it. Okay. No, that'd be very useful. Um, Lam has a question.

Thanks so much for being here today. Um, I wanted to pick up on what you identify as a potentially major design for, and your comments around, you know, what, not wanting to go from a system of ambiguity to potentially wanna more ambiguity. And you pick up on the fact that, you know, there are these two films which will still have to be juggled and I guess navigated in this new system.

What are the implications really, if, if we do get this wrong? And I wanna talk, invite your comments specifically about the timeframes, because you know, you, we were just talking earlier about how we've just started hearings, we've essentially got five more days of hearings and that's it.

And then we are going into the development of this bill to meet the government's timeframes. What is actually the impact if we essentially don't give this the due process and time to actually get it right, and we are just kind of pushing it through this side of election term. Um, if I could start that off.

Um, but there are two things here and I guess, um, one, uh, we're conscious that a lot of the next steps are gonna be handled through national direction, and that kind of does string out the timeframe a little bit. And I think there is some in indication through national direction already. Like, so if you look at the definitions in the NPS infrastructure, I'd actually quite like to see those definition of infrastructure carried through to the definitions within, within the legislation, because that, that, that's already there.

We're actually having a lot of these discussions and I think, you know, we've had the previously proposed RMA replacement as well. So that's one thing. On the risks side of things though, I mean, um, I I think that there are two approval processes, uh, that are, that are kind of, kind of stated with two, two bills here. So would you be able to talk a little bit to that point, Bernie? Um, so, Well, it's simply, I can imagine a situation where it's probably the case already under the RMA today, um, where you get, you know, a permit under one piece

of legislation, but you miss out on the land use of planning consent under the other. Mm-hmm. Um, because the purposes of the two acts are different and, but it's for the same project, you could, you could imagine a subdivision or something like that where you would need all of that whole bundle of consents. Now, you could argue under the RMA today you're applying for a bundle of consents from regional council, also district councils.

And so you could, so the issue there, I'm just wondering though, um, if that's an unforeseen consequence mm-hmm. Of the two bills having two different purposes. So from a contractor's perspective, say if you've got one permit but not the other, you, you, you've, you've incurred costs, you've, you've, you've basically started the project, but you're stuck in limbo for the foreseeable future if you can't get the other permit, you know?

Mm-hmm. So that's, that's a risk. Uh, and that's probably one of the specific risks we wanted to point out. Um, you know, yeah, you're right. Tight, tight timeframe makes it difficult. I think we've had, yeah, I think, I mean, ultimate consequence is gonna be we can't deliver the infrastructure program that is so desperately needed by our communities, ultimately because it gets tied up and delays, it gets tied up in litigation costs escalated.

Um, you know, there's inconsistent interpretations. It's the ambiguity, ambiguity we talk about now. So what we're looking at is the clarity to try and, and avoid those situations. It is expensive, you know, the soil interpretations, $2.4 billion, that's just that one item. Mm-hmm. Imagine how many billions we are losing through delays.

Mm-hmm. Thank you. Rachel Brooking has a question. Yeah. I'm interested in the discussion around activity status. Mm-hmm. Activity classes and the permitted, um, class and then having to pay some more money and, and permitted coming closer to controls.

Mm-hmm. Uh, and of course, this legislation gets rid of the controlled activity status. Yeah. Still has the restricted discretionary and is talking a lot about standards. And you mentioned the appropriateness of standards for some things, um, that contractors do, but, but you also note that some things are more complicated as well. Um, so I'm just wondering on your thoughts if rather than getting into the names of what's permitted and what's controlled and what's restricted discretionary is what you are looking for, clear things that you are allowed

to do if you meet the standards or the rules and the plan, and then some other category that is tightly constrained around what is, what is considered, and, and then a full dis you know, you're always gonna have full discretionary if your project's, It's a really good question.

I wouldn't mind demo from a Yeah. Tractors perspective, Richard. Uh, I guess when you look across the range of works we do as a, as a civil industry, we, we go from, um, you know, so pipeline pipe renewal projects, which will be, um, essentially leading hand, a couple of crew f*****g a digger out doing on the street, doing some pipe placements through to highly complex, you know, complex crane lift, marine works that have got carefully planned engineers on site all the time, pan it through, um, the ones at that end that the kind

of detailed bespoke conditions make sense. But for the ones who are out there less, you know, a lower level of supervision effectively, because it's just a leading hand running the crew, uh, what they want is repeatability. They know what to do, you know, you teach 'em what to do and they do it well. So this is what these guys, these crews are good at. So having clarity for what is permitted, and especially if they can go to and lower heart and upper heart and Wellington and have the same rules, um, exactly what to do is really, really advantageous.

They know how to look after the environment that, that, that's, that's taught. Well, you've got the guideline there of, you know, what we, what we teach our people to do, um, what helps us when they have the same framework Behind it as to what notifications and everything else that goes with it. Thank you for that clarification. And Priyanka, um, thank you for your submission.

I've just got one little question and it's around, um, what you've said in relation to emergency works. Um, and I wondered if you could maybe expand a little bit on what kind of clarity you're looking for in those specific clauses. Are you wanting emergency services listed in, um, in the legislation?

Because I know that you say that most of those provisions are rolled over from the current RMA, um, and that the issue seems to be more in the interpretation of it, rather than the letter of the law specifically. So yeah, so just keen to get some sense of what clarity you're after, but also, um, the extent of the problem as you've experienced it.

Thank you. Yes. Uh, I, I think we are not sure if the provisions are un the purpose or if they may well be, and, but, but we do see attempted prosecution say if there's an emergency response and the contractor moves some dirt off the road, so emergency vehicles can get through, then someone from the council might come and say, you didn't have give us 30 working days of notice.

Say, well, whatever timeframe they would need to, to do that. And, and, and it's actually instance because they were doing it as in part of an emergency response, but the people that are putting forward that, uh, you know, and trying to prosecute based on that haven't understood, then it wastes a lot of time and it's also very useful being told you're going to be prosecuted for, for, for something that doesn't really make any sense. So that's the, the, the practical side of things.

I would say, you're gonna, you gonna unblock the COVID under the rail line, you wanna put it back better, so it's not gonna happen again. Sometimes they go a little bit further because they know that they will prevent future issues. So yeah, there are a few hands-on things when they are directly working for authorities, but I, I think there's also a moral obligation there as well in terms of saving lives and property. So the last time I raised Pacific Committee, the response was, yes, if people are working to save lives

and property, then there is, they should be able to meet that obligation. And the law currently enables that. Why is there a problem? And I thought that was a great response. Its a really interesting response, um, because it's not the response you get from a lot of other people, you know. So I think that's not clear at the moment how the moral obligation to save lives and property, which, if someone has the big equipment there, if they're, they're on site.

I mean, I called everyone after site on Gabrielle and one guy said, you know, um, oh, I asked him hi, and he said, oh, you know, I lost my 16 ton. I lost my, my transporter, but I saved 16 people's lives. So I'm stoked. And, um, amazing. Right? Like, I mean, but um, yeah, I, I think there's, how do you handle those two things together, you know?

So just bringing it back to the reality of the people and what, what happens when there's an emergency Emergency. I think there needs to be some consistancy between the two, both legislation and what we submitted on what the emergency manage call was two things. The first is early engagement with the contractors. So we've actually said we wanna be part of the decision process up front. Now the NZTA response to Cyclone Gabriel was extraordinarily good and that it was on the back of immediately.

They set up a governance group, which included industry and NZTA and an operational group that reported to that group. We had state highway five open in five weeks. There were 38 different things that happened on slips or bru, whatever. Five weeks was an extraordinary result. And the, in the broader response, uh, as soon as the cyclone hit, we did inventory of all of our contractors available and all the equipment that was available.

I turned up the emergency office on day two and said, right here's what we've got. Where do you want us? And they said, we don't need your help. You know, that was the response. It's a fundamental failure of that system, but there has to be the consistent application across both these bits of legislation to make it actually work. Mm-hmm. That's a really good point.

Thank you very much for your submission today. We have met time, um, really valuable submission and, and great to hear the case studies as well in terms into practice. So, uh, we appreciate your time today. Thanks. Thank you very much. Okay, thank you. You and Frazier, can I have a copy of your paper? Yeah, absolutely. Um, Charlie, the Someone will act, that's

Our next submitter, uh, IAG Zealand. Welcome to the Select committee this afternoon. You also have 20 minutes with us, so we will hand it over to you, uh, take your submission As read And uh, yeah, we look forward to you talking us through it. So thank you.

Hi, afternoon all. Um, hello. Some of you, um, I'm Bryce Stab, Andrew Saunders. Um, we'll make some opening remarks. There's a couple of specific points, obviously a few closing remarks, and then open to questions. Um, so thanks for the opportunity. Um, we're a general insurer, uh, so we ensure stuff.

We build generally buildings, factories, homes, all that sort of stuff. Mm. So our interest in this is that, you know, one of the key factors that drives the risk that we underwrite is where a building is and its proximity to natural perils. So where we place buildings in our landscape is of fundamental importance to our ability to ensure them and of fundamental importance to the price we charge people.

So we've always been acutely interested in the Resource Management Act, um, as a key plank and how New Zealand managers natural hazard risk. Um, we have a very complex, um, natural hazard environment in New Zealand when that's becoming more complex. Uh, we have communities that face multiple risks, um, and some of those risks are growing and some of those risks are gonna put the question marks over their ability to stay where they are.

So we need to think very carefully about how we manage those risks. And so this, this bill and the supporting frameworks and, and some, you know, legislation are gonna be really key Part of that, our observation, um, generally is that this bill kind of creates a framework in which we can make good decisions, but it doesn't necessarily require us to do it as a country.

Um, there've been lots of kind of issues that have been raised around the previous or the current Source Management Act. Um, the two bills that were gonna replace it and got the appeal. Um, both of those also created an environment in which we could make good decisions about how we manage natural hazard risk but did not require it.

And so this bill kind of does the same thing. Uh, the success of this bill requires, um, clear system goals, clear national direction about what are an acceptable level of risk, uh, consistent high quality hazard data and models and sufficient capability, um, to be able to implement all of that.

So this is only one piece of a much bigger puzzle for us to get this right as a country. Um, and without the kind of all those supporting elements and this bill being effective, there's no guarantee of achieving better management of risk. Um, and then all of the dire soundings we have about the future and about the future.

So our communities become a little more real. Um, and we note that the key Sorry, can you speak into the microphone a bit? Yeah, I just wanna move that microphone. Um, we can just hear the hallway out there. Oh, right. Sorry. People are quite loud. Yeah, right. Okay. Do I need to repeat anything or shall I keep, no, I'll keep going.

Okay, good. So it, it, you know, from our perspective it kind of lacks a kind of a risk avoidance kind of focus to it, right? And so Andrew, do you wanna talk a little bit about how we see that kind of playing out, particularly in relation to section 11? Well, so section 11, which is kind of the new version I guess, of sections five and six of the RMA and that contains those key objectives for the regime and it contains.

So when we first looked at the bill, we looked great. It's got a clause there on hazards. That's good. That obvious. The RMA did not have that for the first, I don't know, 20 plus years of it's till relatively recently that was included. Um, but we now have one in the RMA and there's one here. And we thought that obviously that's tick one, that's good, there's one there. And we did look at it and we've made some pretty, you know, quite I guess precise points.

We kinda read it quite a few times and we thought we weren't quite sure it was framed in the right way. So we, we are very supportive of that clause being in there. 'cause obviously this section 11 there's gonna be a central park. And we touchstone, as we know with the RMAI can imagine these things could end up in the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court musing about exactly what was meant by these very particular words that were chosen to put on the page as we've seen with sections five and six in the past.

So we thought we had a look at it, we don't, we haven't actually proposed a solution, but we looked at the way it uses the words proportionate and risk-based. It just doesn't, didn't feel quite, the use of the proportionate particular seemed to sit out as almost as a standalone concept rather than being integrated. We don't, I don't think that's necessarily intentional. Uh, and I say we are looking at these words very kind of closely.

We did, I've alluded to, I think we mentioned our submission. We've referenced the, um, the, the quite new national policy statement for natural hazards that came out just at the end of last year, which talks about basically the same thing in policy two, but we think it probably expresses it in a slightly uh, crisp way. It talks about managing things that in a way that's proportionate to the level of national hazard risk, which is what we think Section 11 is trying to say, but we're not quite sure it actually says that.

So that's really just calling out that, I guess we'd commend the committee to have take that away back to the drafters and have a think about whether that, if that is in fact, which is what think is trying to convey. 'cause we're mindful this is a key part. 'cause as price has said yes, it's very framework in legislation. I'm sure you're aware. And this is I guess one of the key within the whole framework and bearing in mind a lot of it will depend on how it's national directions made and how people ultimately plan and consent things.

But this will be a key touchstone. So I guess we're very focused on getting that one, getting that one bit right and making clear that how risks should be it should be managed. But that's just to unpack what we on that first kind of specific point. Yeah. So, so the act overall kind of includes and we look at the things that we want to see.
